context.project_description: Extend the existing instrumentation framework to track LLM token usage for ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider, and VisionProvider operations, enabling observability into how many tokens each query consumes.
key_requirements: Add token accounting capability that tracks input/output tokens per LLM call
Implement approximate token counting using text length and model context windows
Integrate with existing BenchmarkHook infrastructure without breaking API compatibility
constraints: Only instrument ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider, and VisionProvider operations (not RerankProvider)
Preserve backward compatibility - do not modify existing APIs or response formats
Leverage existing InstrumentedProxy pattern rather than creating new wrapper classes
stakeholders: user who wants to see how many LLM tokens each query costs
engine components that use ChatProvider, EmbeddingProvider, VisionProvider

in_scope: token estimation based on text length and model context windows
out_of_scope: modifying API responses to include token counts
implementing cost estimator based on token counts
real-time streaming metrics for token usage

existing_files: fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py — Instrumentation system for benchmarking plugin performance.
fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py — Provider protocols for LLM clients.
fitz_ai/llm/providers/openai.py — OpenAI provider wrappers using the official SDK.
fitz_ai/llm/providers/anthropic.py — Anthropic provider wrappers using the official SDK.
fitz_ai/llm/providers/cohere.py — Cohere provider wrappers using the official SDK.
fitz_ai/llm/providers/ollama.py — Ollama provider wrappers using direct HTTP calls.
needed_artifacts: fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py — Extend existing infrastructure to support token tracking by adding a new hook interface specifically for token accounting and updating InstrumentedProxy logic to handle token counters properly

assumptions.assumption: Token Counting Methodology: We approximate tokens using text length and model context windows, since exact counting requires access to each provider's tokenizer which is not exposed in current SDKs.
assumptions.impact: Approximate cost tracking; acceptable for observability but may lead to inaccurate billing estimates.

assumptions.assumption: Instrumentation Integration: Existing instrumentation framework via `BenchmarkHook` can be extended to track LLM calls with token usage metrics.
assumptions.impact: Some provider implementations may require custom handling beyond the generic approach, potentially increasing complexity or breaking compatibility.

assumptions.assumption: Provider Support Scope: Only Chat, Embedding, and Vision operations are instrumented as these represent core LLM usage for queries.
assumptions.impact: Rerank could also incur costs but is not tracked, possibly missing significant cost data.

assumptions.assumption: Token Accounting Granularity: Per-call token tracking (input/output) suffices for the requirement to 'see how many LLM tokens each query costs'.
assumptions.impact: Streaming operations or high-frequency calls may go untracked if not handled correctly, leading to incomplete cost visibility.

assumptions.assumption: API Compatibility: Token information should be logged/observed without changing API responses.
assumptions.impact: If users want token data returned in response objects, changes would be needed which could break backward compatibility.

assumptions.assumption: Token Definition Consistency: For cost accounting purposes, chat uses input + output tokens as 'total tokens', embeddings count text as input, and vision assumes fixed estimates for image processing.
assumptions.impact: Providers may define tokens differently internally, leading to inconsistencies in cost reporting across different LLM backends.

approaches.name: Token Accounting Hook Pattern
approaches.description: A new `TokenAccountingHook` protocol extending `BenchmarkHook`, updated `InstrumentedProxy._wrap_method()` to track tokens when hooks are present, new token estimation logic using model context windows and text length approximations, integration with existing instrumentation system (no API changes), token tracking in `on_call_start`/`on_call_end` methods of the hook
pros: Integrates cleanly with existing infrastructure without breaking changes
Leverages established `BenchmarkHook` pattern already in use
Extensible to all providers with minimal code duplication
Requires no API modifications and maintains backward compatibility
Unified token tracking across multiple providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama)
cons: Approximation approach has variance that could be misleading
Relies on model context windows which may not exactly match actual token usage
best_for: Multi-provider LLM systems needing unified metrics

approaches.name: Token Counting Service Pattern
approaches.description: Dedicated `TokenCounterService` that wraps provider instances, injected via dependency injection throughout the system, requires modifying factory methods and service constructors to inject this counter, new service must be passed through entire call stack
pros: Clear separation of concerns between core logic and token accounting
Explicit control over when and where token counting occurs
cons: Requires extensive refactoring of existing systems
Breaking API contracts due to constructor signature changes
High operational burden from updating factory methods and usage sites
Potential for breaking changes throughout the system
best_for: Systems where clear separation is more important than minimal impact
Projects with flexibility in API contracts

architecture.reasoning: Approach 1 is correct because it integrates cleanly with existing infrastructure without breaking changes, leverages the established BenchmarkHook pattern already in use, and is extensible to all providers with minimal code duplication. It requires no API modifications and maintains backward compatibility while enabling unified token tracking across multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama). Approach 2 would be wrong because it requires extensive refactoring that violates the constraint of not modifying existing APIs or response formats, potentially breaking many systems. Approach 3 would be wrong because it's error-prone and inconsistent compared to the existing proxy-based instrumentation system with manual application across all provider implementations.

key_tradeoffs.Instrumentation vs Refactoring: Approach 1 uses existing hook infrastructure with minimal changes, while Approach 2 requires extensive system refactoring that breaks API contracts
key_tradeoffs.Precision vs Performance: Approximate token counting based on text length and context windows trades exactness for performance and simplicity
key_tradeoffs.Coverage Consistency: Decorator pattern risks inconsistent coverage across providers, while hook pattern ensures uniform tracking

technology_considerations: TokenAccountingHook protocol extending BenchmarkHook for seamless integration with existing instrumentation system
InstrumentedProxy._wrap_method() modification to detect and invoke token accounting hooks without API changes
Text length and context window approximations for token estimation across multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama)
ValueError raising for unsupported model context windows instead of silent failure to ensure robustness
architecture.scope_statement: Implement token accounting via a new hook protocol extending BenchmarkHook that integrates with InstrumentedProxy without API changes, using approximation methods for text length and context windows.

adrs.title: ADR: Token Accounting Hook Pattern
adrs.context: Need to track token usage across different LLM providers (Chat, Embedding, Vision) without breaking existing APIs or requiring extensive refactoring.
adrs.decision: Implement a new TokenAccountingHook protocol that extends BenchmarkHook and integrate it into the existing InstrumentedProxy system for automatic instrumentation of provider methods.
adrs.rationale: This approach leverages the existing benchmarking infrastructure, maintains backward compatibility, requires no API changes, and scales across all supported providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama). It avoids heavy refactoring needed by service or decorator patterns.
consequences: Minimal operational burden - only adds new hook type and modifies existing wrapping logic
Unified token tracking across multiple provider implementations
Integration with existing benchmarking infrastructure without API modifications
Approximate token counting based on text length and context windows (not exact parsing)
Excludes RerankProvider from tracking per requirements
alternatives_considered: Service Pattern - rejected because it requires extensive refactoring of constructor signatures and service injection points, violating API contract constraints
Decorator Pattern - rejected because it's error-prone with inconsistent coverage across providers and requires manual application everywhere

adrs.title: ADR: Token Data Granularity vs. Performance Tradeoff
adrs.context: The TokenAccountingHook architecture must decide what level of token detail to capture and propagate across system boundaries, particularly when instrumenting proxy requests for multiple LLM providers.
adrs.decision: Capture only essential token counts (input/output) rather than detailed per-token breakdowns or timing data in the hook interface
adrs.rationale: This approach minimizes cross-boundary data transfer while maintaining core functionality for billing and analytics. The detailed tokenization information can be retained internally within each provider's instrumentation layer.
consequences: Reduced observability for debugging specific prompt/response token usage patterns
Potential loss of fine-grained cost optimization opportunities that require per-token analysis
Simpler hook interface contract with fewer fields to maintain across different providers
alternatives_considered: Passing complete tokenization metadata through hooks including per-token costs and timing data
Implementing a pluggable telemetry format that can be configured for different provider requirements

components.name: TokenAccountingHook
components.purpose: Extends BenchmarkHook to provide token tracking capability for LLM method calls
responsibilities: Track input and output tokens for chat, embed, and describe_image operations
Provide approximate token estimation based on text length and model context windows
interfaces: on_call_start(layer: str, plugin_name: str, method: str, args: tuple, kwargs: dict) -> Any
on_call_end(context: Any, result: Any, error: Exception | None) -> None

components.name: InstrumentedProxy
components.purpose: Wraps provider instances to instrument method calls with hooks
responsibilities: Wrap target objects with instrumentation for tracking
Detect and invoke token accounting hooks during method execution
Maintain proxy behavior while adding timing and hook invocation
interfaces: __init__(target: Any, layer: str, plugin_name: str, methods_to_track: set[str] | None)

integration_points: InstrumentedProxy — extends BenchmarkHook protocol to track token usage during LLM calls, integrating with existing benchmarking infrastructure

artifacts.content:
class BenchmarkHook(Protocol):
  on_call_start(layer: str, plugin_name: str, method: str, args: tuple, kwargs: dict) -> Any
  on_call_end(context: Any, result: Any, error: Exception | None) -> None

class CachingHook(Protocol):
  get_cached_result(layer: str, plugin_name: str, method: str, args: tuple, kwargs: dict) -> Any
  cache_result(layer: str, plugin_name: str, method: str, args: tuple, kwargs: dict, result: Any) -> None

class TokenAccountingHook(Protocol):
  on_call_start(layer: str, plugin_name: str, method: str, args: tuple, kwargs: dict) -> Any
  on_call_end(context: Any, result: Any, error: Exception | None) -> None
  record_input_tokens(layer: str, plugin_name: str, method: str, tokens: int) -> None
  record_output_tokens(layer: str, plugin_name: str, method: str, tokens: int) -> None

register_hook(hook: BenchmarkHook) -> None
unregister_hook(hook: BenchmarkHook) -> None
clear_hooks() -> None
has_hooks() -> bool
get_hooks() -> list[BenchmarkHook]

class InstrumentedProxy:
  __init__(target: Any, layer: str, plugin_name: str, methods_to_track: set[str] | None)
  __getattr__(name: str) -> Any
  _wrap_method(method: Callable, method_name: str) -> Callable
  __repr__() -> str
  __str__() -> str

maybe_wrap(target: Any, layer: str, plugin_name: str, methods_to_track: set[str] | None) -> Any
wrap(target: Any, layer: str, plugin_name: str, methods_to_track: set[str] | None) -> InstrumentedProxy

  on_call_end(context: Any, result: Any, error: Exception | None) -> None

artifacts.purpose: Instrumentation system for benchmarking plugin performance including new token accounting hook protocol and updates to proxy wrapping logic

phases.name: Read Before Writing
phases.objective: Verify integration points by reading source

phases.name: Define TokenAccountingHook Protocol
phases.objective: Create the new hook interface to extend benchmarking capabilities for token tracking
key_risks: Risk of missing existing infrastructure patterns in the codebase

phases.name: Enhance InstrumentedProxy Logic
phases.objective: Update proxy logic to support both BenchmarkHook and TokenAccountingHook
deliverables: Updated _wrap_method in fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py to handle token accounting hooks
key_risks: Risk of not properly integrating with existing InstrumentedProxy._wrap_method implementation
Potential performance overhead from additional hook checks

phases.name: Create Sample Hook Implementation
phases.objective: Create a simple hook that demonstrates usage for testing purposes
deliverables: MockTokenHook implementation in tests or example code
key_risks: Risk of incomplete mock implementation that doesn't cover edge cases

risks.description: Hook Registration Timing - The implementation depends on proper registration of hooks before providers are instrumented
risks.mitigation: Implement thorough testing to verify that registering hook after provider creation still works properly through instrumentation checks
risks.contingency: If late registration fails, implement a fallback mechanism that attempts re-instrumentation when hooks are registered later
risks.verification: Test that registering hook after provider creation still works properly (through instrumentation checks)

risks.description: Provider Method Signatures - Different LLM providers have varying method signatures causing potential instrumentation gaps
risks.mitigation: Test with each provider type (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) to ensure all methods are instrumented correctly
risks.contingency: For uninstrumented methods, log a warning and implement graceful fallback behavior while documenting unsupported methods
risks.verification: Test with each provider type (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama)

risks.description: Streaming Chat Methods - chat_stream methods return iterators rather than strings making token tracking complex
risks.mitigation: Implement separate handling for streaming vs non-streaming calls to ensure correct token estimation for both cases
risks.contingency: For streaming responses, implement approximate token counting that tracks cumulative output tokens over time rather than individual chunks
risks.verification: Test streaming vs non-streaming calls to verify proper handling of both types

risks.description: Batch Operations - EmbeddingProvider has batch operations that process multiple texts requiring correct aggregation of token counts
risks.mitigation: Create unit tests on embed_batch and related methods with multiple items to verify proper token counting across batches
risks.contingency: If batch aggregation fails, implement conservative estimate approach that overcounts tokens rather than undercounting
risks.verification: Unit tests on embed_batch and related methods with multiple items

risks.description: Instrumentation Overhead - Adding hooks may introduce performance overhead to already slow LLM calls
risks.mitigation: Conduct performance benchmarks showing <10% overhead in typical usage and optimize hook execution logic where possible
risks.contingency: Implement optional instrumentation flag that allows disabling token tracking when not needed for development/testing
risks.verification: Performance benchmarks showing <10% overhead in typical usage

risks.description: Hook Registration Dependencies - Consumers may use token tracking without proper hook setup causing silent failure
risks.mitigation: Implement comprehensive testing for both cases: with and without hooks registered to ensure graceful degradation
risks.contingency: Add explicit validation that ensures hooks are registered before attempting token tracking, or implement fallback behavior when no hooks present
risks.verification: Test both cases: with and without hooks registered

risks.description: Token estimation fails silently for unknown models or edge cases
risks.mitigation: Add default fallbacks (e.g., conservative estimates) that are logged as warnings when token estimation cannot be performed accurately
risks.contingency: Implement comprehensive logging with warning levels to alert users about potential inaccuracies in token tracking for unknown models
risks.verification: Add default fallbacks (e.g., conservative estimates) that are logged as warnings

recommended_contingencies: Implement a fallback mechanism for late hook registration that attempts re-instrumentation when hooks become available
For uninstrumented methods, implement graceful logging and documentation of unsupported methods instead of complete failure
Establish separate handling logic for streaming vs non-streaming responses to properly track token usage over time
Create conservative estimate approach for batch operations that overcounts tokens rather than undercounting in case of aggregation failures
Implement optional instrumentation flag that allows disabling token tracking when not needed during development/testing cycles
